title: ser vs estar (to be)
explanationMd: Portuguese has two verbs for 'to be'. **ser** is for permanent/defining traits (identity, origin, profession, time). **estar** is for temporary states (location of people, mood, current condition). The intuition that unlocks it: ser answers *what something is*, estar answers *how or where it is right now*. Brazilians exploit the contrast for meaning — *ela é linda* (she's a beautiful person) vs *ela está linda* (she looks great today). In fast speech **estar** gets clipped: *tô*, *tá*, *tamos*, *tão* — you will hear *tô cansado* far more often than *estou cansado*, and using the clipped forms yourself is normal, not sloppy.

examples.translation: I am Brazilian. (identity → ser)
I am tired. (temporary → estar)
I'm at home. (spoken clipped estar)
The coffee is hot. (current condition → estar)

commonErrors.note: Tiredness is temporary → estar.
Location of a person → estar.

drills.prompt: Say 'the coffee is cold' the way Brazilians do (clipped).
'She is a doctor' — ser or estar?

title: Present tense — regular -ar verbs
explanationMd: Regular **-ar** verbs (like *falar*) take the endings -o, -a, -amos, -am in the present. That is the whole paradigm you need for spoken Brazil: because *você*, *ele/ela* and *a gente* all use the same third-person singular form, everyday speech really runs on just three forms — *falo*, *fala*, *falam* (plus *falamos* if you say *nós*). The Brazilian present also covers English's present continuous in many contexts (*trabalho no centro* = 'I work / I'm working downtown') and even the near future with a time word: *falo com ele amanhã* — 'I'll talk to him tomorrow'.

examples.text: Eu falo, você fala, nós falamos, eles falam.
examples.translation: I speak, you speak, we speak, they speak.

examples.text: A gente fala português em casa.
examples.translation: We speak Portuguese at home. (a gente + singular verb)
I'll call you tomorrow. (present used for near future)

commonErrors.note: 1st person singular ends in -o.
Você takes the third-person form, never -s.

title: você vs tu vs o senhor (you)
explanationMd: Most of Brazil uses **você** for 'you', and it takes third-person verb forms — so learning você costs you nothing extra: it conjugates like *ele/ela*. Some regions (the South, Rio, much of the North and Northeast) use **tu**, but crucially most tu-speakers pair it with the third-person verb anyway: *tu fala*, *tu vai* — textbook *tu falas* sounds bookish or specifically gaúcho/maranhense. **O senhor / a senhora** is the respectful form for elders, clients, police, or anyone you'd call 'sir/ma'am'; using it with a peer sounds distant, but skipping it with a 70-year-old can sound rude. In texting, você shrinks to **vc** or even just **cê** in speech: *cê vai?*

examples.translation: Do you speak English? (neutral, standard BR)
Do you need help, sir? (formal)
Are you going there today? (regional tu + 3rd-person verb, very common)
You want coffee? (spoken reduction of você)

commonErrors.note: Você always takes third-person forms.
Use the formal form with elders and in service situations until invited otherwise.

drills.prompt: Ask a hotel receptionist's elderly guest if he needs water, politely.
How do most Brazilian tu-speakers actually say 'you speak'?

steps.prompt: Ask a friend casually if they want coffee, using você.
Ask an elderly stranger politely if they want coffee.
steps.note: formal register with o senhor/a senhora

steps.prompt: Ask the same in the spoken, reduced form of você.

title: Articles and gender (o/a, um/uma)
explanationMd: Every Portuguese noun is masculine or feminine, and the article carries that information: **o/a** (the), **um/uma** (a/an). The -o = masculine, -a = feminine pattern covers most nouns, but memorize the article *with* the noun because the exceptions are frequent words: *o dia* (day), *o problema*, *o mapa* are masculine; *a mão* (hand), *a foto* are feminine. Unlike English, Brazilians use the definite article before possessives and often before people's names: *o meu carro*, *a Maria chegou* — dropping it there sounds foreign, not cleaner. Gender errors rarely block understanding, but they are the single loudest 'gringo marker', so drill article + noun as one chunk.

examples.text: o pão, a água, um café, uma casa
examples.translation: the bread, the water, a coffee, a house

examples.text: O problema é o dia da viagem.
examples.translation: The problem is the day of the trip. (both masculine despite -a / looking odd)

examples.text: A Ana é a minha amiga.
examples.translation: Ana is my friend. (article before name and possessive — standard spoken BR)

commonErrors.note: Greek-origin -ma nouns are masculine: o problema, o sistema, o cinema.
Possessives agree with the noun's gender: casa is feminine.

title: Plural formation
explanationMd: The default plural is add **-s** (*casa → casas*). Words ending in -r or -z add **-es** (*mulher → mulheres*), -m becomes **-ns** (*homem → homens*), and **-ão** splits three ways: usually **-ões** (*coração → corações*), sometimes **-ães** (*pão → pães*), rarely just **-ãos** (*mão → mãos*) — learn those case by case. Here is the spoken-Brazil reality: in casual speech many people mark the plural only on the article and drop it on the noun — *os amigo*, *as casa*. Understand it when you hear it, but produce the full agreement yourself (*os amigos*); the reduced form is heavily stigmatized in writing and formal contexts.

examples.text: a casa → as casas; o amigo → os amigos
examples.translation: the house → the houses; the friend → the friends
the bread (loaf) → the breads (loaves)

examples.text: o homem → os homens; a mulher → as mulheres
examples.translation: the man → the men; the woman → the women

commonErrors.note: pão belongs to the -ão → -ães group.
Final -m becomes -ns in the plural.

title: Present tense — regular -er and -ir verbs
explanationMd: Regular **-er** verbs (*comer*) take -o, -e, -emos, -em; regular **-ir** verbs (*abrir*) take -o, -e, -imos, -em. Notice the two families differ only in the *nós* form (-emos vs -imos) — everything you actually use daily with você/ele/a gente is identical, so treat them as one pattern with a footnote. As with -ar verbs, spoken Brazil leans on three forms: *como, come, comem*. Watch out for first-person stem changes in some common -ir verbs even when the rest is regular: *dormir → eu durmo*, *subir → eu subo* (regular) but *pedir → eu peço*.

examples.text: Eu como pão, ela come pão, eles comem pão.
examples.translation: I eat bread, she eats bread, they eat bread.
Do you drink coffee every day?

examples.text: A gente abre a loja às nove.
examples.translation: We open the store at nine.

commonErrors.note: 1st person singular ends in -o for all three conjugations.
-er verbs take -emos; comimos is the past tense.

title: Key irregular verbs: ter, ir, fazer, poder, querer
explanationMd: Five irregulars carry a huge share of daily conversation, so memorize them as whole words, not rules: **ter** (tenho, tem, temos, têm), **ir** (vou, vai, vamos, vão), **fazer** (faço, faz, fazemos, fazem), **poder** (posso, pode, podemos, podem), **querer** (quero, quer, queremos, querem). They punch above their weight because Brazilians build other structures on them: *ter* doubles as 'there is' (*tem café?*), *ir* builds the spoken future (*vou fazer*), *poder* and *querer* chain directly onto infinitives (*posso pagar?*, *quero comer*). Note *tem* vs *têm* differ only in writing — they sound identical, so in speech context does the work.

examples.text: Tenho dois amigos em São Paulo.
examples.translation: I have two friends in São Paulo.
She makes coffee every morning.
Can I drink this water?
I want bread, please.

commonErrors.note: ter is irregular: tenho, not a regular -er form.
querer drops the -e in third person singular.
fazer: first person is faço, with ç.

title: estar + gerund (tô fazendo)
explanationMd: Brazil's present continuous is **estar + -ndo**: *estou falando* (I'm speaking), *está comendo* (he's eating). Form the gerund by swapping the infinitive's -r for **-ndo**: falar → falando, comer → comendo, abrir → abrindo — no exceptions worth learning at this level. This is a genuinely Brazilian trait: European Portuguese says *estou a falar* instead, so the -ndo form instantly marks you as Brazil-oriented. In real speech estar is clipped, giving the extremely common *tô fazendo*, *tá chovendo*, *tão chegando*. Use it for actions in progress right now or current ongoing phases (*tô estudando português* — I'm studying Portuguese these days).

examples.text: Tô comendo agora, te ligo depois.
examples.translation: I'm eating right now, I'll call you later.

examples.text: Ela tá falando com a mãe.
examples.translation: She's talking with her mom.
We're living on the same street.

commonErrors.note: 'estar a + infinitive' is European Portuguese; Brazil uses the -ndo gerund.
The main verb must be in the -ndo form after estar.

title: ir + infinitive — the future Brazilians actually speak
explanationMd: Forget the conjugated future tense (*falarei*, *comerá*) for speaking — Brazilians almost never say it; it survives mainly in news writing, contracts, and horoscopes. The spoken future is **ir (conjugated) + infinitive**: *vou falar* (I'll speak), *vai chover* (it's going to rain), *vamos comer* (we're going to eat). One quirk: for 'going to go', Brazilians just say **vou** — *vou lá amanhã*, not *vou ir lá*. Since you already know vou/vai/vamos/vão, you now have the entire future tense for free. Add a time word (*amanhã*, *depois*, *hoje à noite*) and you're done.

examples.translation: I'm going to talk to him today.

examples.text: A gente vai comer fora amanhã.
examples.translation: We're going to eat out tomorrow.
I'll go there later. (not 'vou ir')

commonErrors.note: The -rei future sounds stiff/written; use ir + infinitive in speech.
For 'going to go', ir alone carries the future — though you WILL hear 'vou ir' colloquially, avoid it.

drills.prompt: Say 'she is going to drink water'.
Say 'I'll go to the house tomorrow' (no double ir).

title: Negation — não before the verb (and não... não)
explanationMd: Basic negation is simple: put **não** directly before the verb — *não falo*, *não quero*. Nothing comes between them except object pronouns (*não te vi*). Spoken Brazil then adds two twists. First, the double negative **não... não** for emphasis or soft refusal: *não quero, não* — completely standard in speech, never wrong-sounding. Second, in fast informal speech the *first* não can disappear, leaving only the final one: *quero não* — very common in the Northeast. Also note negative answers: Brazilians rarely answer yes/no questions with a bare *sim*; they echo the verb — *Você fala inglês? Falo. / Não falo, não.*

examples.translation: I don't drink coffee at night.
I don't want to. (soft, natural refusal)

examples.text: — Você come carne? — Como. / — Não como, não.
examples.translation: — Do you eat meat? — I do. / — No, I don't. (echoing the verb instead of sim/não)

commonErrors.note: não goes immediately before the verb.
Verb-echo answers are the natural Brazilian yes.

drills.prompt: Refuse softly, the spoken-BR way: 'I don't want to.'
Answer 'Você bebe café?' affirmatively the natural way.

title: Question formation — intonation and question words
explanationMd: This is the easiest part of Portuguese: there is no word-order change and no auxiliary like English 'do'. A yes/no question is just the statement with rising intonation: *Você fala inglês?* Question words go up front and the order still doesn't flip: **o que** (what), **quem** (who), **onde** (where), **quando** (when), **por que** (why), **como** (how), **quanto** (how much/many), **qual** (which). Two spoken-Brazil habits to recognize: tacking **é que** after the question word (*onde é que você mora?* — pure emphasis, no meaning change) and echoing the question word at the end with 'o quê?' style stress (*você quer o quê?*). Rhetorical tag: *né?* (= não é?) turns anything into 'right?'.

examples.translation: Do you drink coffee? (statement + rising intonation)
Where do you live? (no inversion)

examples.text: Por que é que ele não veio?
examples.translation: Why didn't he come? (emphatic 'é que')

examples.text: Você é o amigo do João, né?
examples.translation: You're João's friend, right?

commonErrors.note: No subject-verb inversion in Portuguese questions.
Keep normal word order after the question word.

drills.prompt: Turn 'Você quer água.' into a question.

drills.answer: Onde fica a rua? (or: Onde é a rua?)

drills.prompt: Add the Brazilian tag 'right?' to 'Você fala português'.

title: Contractions: no/na, do/da, num/numa, pra/pro
explanationMd: Prepositions fuse with articles, and these fusions are mandatory, not optional style: **em + o/a = no/na** (in the), **de + o/a = do/da** (of/from the), **a + o/a = ao/à** (to the), **em + um/uma = num/numa** (in a). Saying *em a casa* is simply wrong. Then there's the all-spoken star: **para** reduces to **pra**, and fuses further to **pro/pra** (para o / para a) — *vou pro trabalho*, *isso é pra você*. You will hear *pra* in virtually every Brazilian sentence; full *para* in speech sounds like reading aloud. Write *para* in formal text, say *pra* everywhere else. Plurals work the same: nos/nas, dos/das, uns → nuns, etc.

examples.translation: The milk is in the fridge. (em + a = na)
He's from Rio. (de + o = do)
I go to work on foot. (para o = pro)
I live on a quiet street. (em + uma = numa)

commonErrors.note: em + article must contract: no/na/num/numa.
de + o contracts to do.

drills.prompt: Say 'this is for you' the spoken way.

title: Possessives: meu/minha, seu/sua, dele/dela
explanationMd: Possessives agree with the thing owned, not the owner: **meu/minha** (my), **seu/sua** (your), **nosso/nossa** (our). Here's the trap: textbook *seu* also means 'his/her', but since você took over the third-person forms, *seu carro* is ambiguous — your car or his car? Brazilians resolved it decisively: in speech **seu/sua = your (você's)**, and 'his/her' is expressed with **dele/dela** placed *after* the noun: *o carro dele* (his car), *a casa dela* (her house). Use dele/dela and you'll never be misunderstood. Also remember spoken BR normally keeps the article before possessives: *o meu amigo*, *a minha casa*.

examples.translation: My house is nearby.

examples.text: O carro dele tá na rua.
examples.translation: His car is on the street. (dele = unambiguous 'his')
Your coffee is getting cold. (seu = your in spoken BR)
Her friend lives here.

commonErrors.note: Agree with the possessed noun: casa is feminine.
In spoken BR 'seu' will be heard as 'your'; say 'dele/dela' for his/her.

drills.prompt: Say 'my friend (male)' with the article, spoken style.

title: Demonstratives: esse, este, aquele
explanationMd: The textbook system is three-way: **este** (near me), **esse** (near you), **aquele** (far from both), each with feminine (-a) and neutral (isto/isso/aquilo) forms. Spoken Brazil collapsed it to two: **esse** covers both 'this' and 'that near you' — *este* survives mostly in writing and set phrases. So in conversation you need only **esse/essa/isso** (this/that here-ish) vs **aquele/aquela/aquilo** (that over there). The neutral forms isso/aquilo are for unnamed things and ideas: *o que é isso?* (what's that?), *isso!* (exactly!/that's it! — a hugely common affirmation). Combine with contractions: *nesse*, *nessa*, *naquele*, *disso*, *daquilo*.

examples.translation: This bread is nice and fresh. (spoken 'esse' where books say 'este')

examples.text: Aquela casa é do meu amigo.
examples.translation: That house (over there) is my friend's.
What is that/this? (neutral isso for unnamed things)

examples.text: — Então a gente se vê amanhã? — Isso!
examples.translation: — So we'll see each other tomorrow? — Exactly!

commonErrors.note: Not an error, but 'este' sounds written/formal; spoken BR uses esse for near things.

commonErrors.wrong: esse aqui vs aquele confusion: using 'esse' for something far away
commonErrors.note: Distance still matters: far = aquele/aquela/aquilo.

drills.prompt: Say 'this coffee is good' the spoken way.
Ask 'what is that?' about an unknown object.

title: Object pronouns in spoken Brazil (me/te fronting, o/a avoidance)
explanationMd: This is where spoken Brazil breaks hardest with the textbook. First, placement: Brazilians put **me/te/se/nos** *before* the verb, even to start a sentence — *te amo*, *me ajuda*, *me passa o sal* — whereas European grammar demands *amo-te*. Nobody in Brazil says *amo-te* in conversation. Second, the third-person clitics **o/a** ('him/her/it') are practically dead in speech: instead Brazilians repeat the noun, use **ele/ela** after the verb (*eu vi ela ontem* — technically 'wrong', universally said), or just drop the object entirely when it's obvious (*— Comprou o pão? — Comprei.*). Learn to recognize o/a/lo/la in writing, but don't build your speech on them.

examples.translation: I love you. (pronoun fronted — standard spoken BR)
Help me with this? (starting with 'me' is normal in Brazil)

examples.text: Eu vi ela na rua hoje.
examples.translation: I saw her on the street today. (spoken; written norm would be 'eu a vi')
— Did you drink the coffee? — I did. (object simply dropped)

commonErrors.note: Verb-attached pronouns sound European/literary; Brazilians front them.
Fine in writing, stiff in speech — spoken BR uses ele/ela as object or drops it.

drills.prompt: Say 'call me tomorrow' the Brazilian way.
Say 'I saw her today' the spoken way.
Answer '— Comeu o pão?' the natural way (yes).

title: gostar de — liking things and doing things
explanationMd: **Gostar** always takes **de** before its object — no exceptions: *gosto de café*, *gosto de você*. With verbs, use de + infinitive: *gosto de comer fora* (I like eating out). The 'de' contracts with articles and pronouns: *gosto do pão daqui*, *gosto dele*. English speakers drop the de constantly; Brazilians will still understand you, but it's a persistent error worth killing early. In fast speech *de* often reduces to a 'dji' sound (*gosto dji café*) — that's pronunciation, not a different word. For 'would like' (polite wanting), Brazilians usually say **queria**: *queria um café* — literally imperfect of querer, functioning as 'I'd like'.

examples.translation: I like coffee without milk.

examples.text: Ela gosta de falar de comida.
examples.translation: She likes talking about food.
Do you like him? (de + ele = dele)
I'd like a bread roll, please.

commonErrors.note: gostar always needs de.
de + o contracts to do.

title: Pretérito perfeito — regular past (-ar/-er/-ir)
explanationMd: The perfeito is the workhorse past: completed actions, one-time events, 'what happened'. Regular endings — **-ar**: falei, falou, falamos, falaram; **-er**: comi, comeu, comemos, comeram; **-ir**: abri, abriu, abrimos, abriram. Good news: -er and -ir are identical in the past. Two things matter for real usage. First, the perfeito also covers English's present perfect: *já comi* = 'I've already eaten' — Portuguese does NOT use its compound tense for this. Second, note *falamos* is both 'we speak' and 'we spoke'; context (or a time word like *ontem*) disambiguates, and Brazilians don't even notice the ambiguity.

examples.translation: I spoke with her yesterday.
Did you eat all the bread?
I've already had my coffee today. (perfeito = English present perfect)

commonErrors.wrong: Eu tenho comido o pão. (for 'I ate/I've eaten the bread')
commonErrors.note: Don't calque English 'have eaten'; the simple perfeito covers it. ('Tenho comido' means a repeated recent habit.)
-ei for eu, -ou for você/ele.

drills.prompt: Say 'she spoke with the friend yesterday'.
drills.answer: Ela falou com o amigo ontem.

title: Irregular past: fui, fiz, tive, disse, pude, quis
explanationMd: The most-used verbs have irregular pasts, and they're unavoidable in any story about your day: **ser/ir share the same forms** (fui, foi, fomos, foram — context tells you 'was' vs 'went'), **fazer** → fiz, fez, fizemos, fizeram; **ter** → tive, teve, tivemos, tiveram; **dizer** → disse, disse, dissemos, disseram; **poder** → pude, pôde, pudemos, puderam; **querer** → quis, quis, quisemos, quiseram; **estar** → estive, esteve. Don't try to derive these — chunk-memorize the eu and você/ele forms first, since those carry most conversations. The ser/ir merger surprises learners but never confuses Brazilians: *fui ao Rio* (I went), *fui professor* (I was).

examples.text: Ontem eu fui na casa dela.
examples.translation: Yesterday I went to her house. (ir → fui)
The party was great. (ser → foi — same form as 'went')
I made coffee for everyone.

examples.text: Ele disse que não teve tempo.
examples.translation: He said he didn't have time.

commonErrors.note: fazer is irregular in the past: fiz, fez.
ter → tive, teve.
dizer → disse (same form for eu and ele).

title: Imperfeito vs perfeito — background vs event
explanationMd: The imperfeito paints the background; the perfeito reports the event. **Imperfeito** = habits ('used to'), ongoing states, descriptions, and 'was doing': -ar verbs take -ava (falava, falávamos), -er/-ir take -ia (comia, abria). Only four irregulars matter: ser (era), ter (tinha), ir (ia), vir (vinha). The classic combo: *eu **estava** comendo quando ela **chegou*** — background (imperfeito) interrupted by event (perfeito). Two very Brazilian uses to know: **tinha** replaces 'havia' for 'there was/were' in speech (*tinha muita gente*), and the imperfeito works as a polite conditional: *queria um café* (I'd like), *podia me ajudar?* (could you help me?) — far more common in speech than the true conditional.

examples.text: Quando eu era criança, morava numa rua pequena.
examples.translation: When I was a kid, I lived (used to live) on a small street.

examples.text: Eu tava bebendo café quando você ligou.
examples.translation: I was drinking coffee when you called. (imperfeito background + perfeito event)
There were a lot of people at the party. (spoken 'there was' in the past)

examples.text: Ela sempre comia pão de manhã.
examples.translation: She always used to eat bread in the morning.

commonErrors.note: Age/description in the past is background → imperfeito.

commonErrors.wrong: Eu bebi café quando você ligou (meaning 'was drinking')
commonErrors.right: Eu estava/tava bebendo café quando você ligou.
commonErrors.note: Interrupted ongoing action → imperfeito (or estava + gerund).

drills.prompt: Say 'there was water in the house' (spoken).
'I used to drink milk every day.'

title: a gente = we (with singular verb)
explanationMd: In everyday Brazil, 'we' is usually **a gente**, not *nós* — and it takes the **third-person singular** verb, exactly like você: *a gente fala*, *a gente vai*, *a gente foi*. Literally 'the people', it grammaticalized into a pronoun; *nós* isn't wrong, just more formal or emphatic, and it drags along the -mos endings you then have to conjugate. Big payoff for learners: a gente lets you talk about 'us' using forms you already know. Watch agreement traps: the verb is singular even though the meaning is plural (*a gente foi*, never *a gente fomos* — though you'll hear that 'error' from natives), and adjectives agree with the speakers' reality: *a gente tava cansado/cansada*.

examples.text: A gente mora na mesma rua.
examples.translation: We live on the same street.

examples.text: A gente vai comer fora hoje.
examples.translation: We're going to eat out today.

examples.text: Ontem a gente falou sobre isso.
examples.translation: Yesterday we talked about that.

commonErrors.note: a gente takes the singular verb form (like você).

commonErrors.right: A gente é amigo. / Nós somos amigos.
commonErrors.note: Keep verb singular with a gente; switch to nós if you want -mos forms.

drills.prompt: Say 'we drink coffee every day' with 'a gente'.
drills.answer: A gente bebe café todo dia.

drills.prompt: Say 'we went to the house' with 'a gente'.
Conjugation rule: a gente takes which verb form?

title: ficar — the swiss-army verb
explanationMd: **Ficar** is one of the highest-value verbs in Brazilian Portuguese because it does at least four jobs. (1) Permanent location of places — where estar handles people, ficar handles buildings: *a casa fica na rua principal*. (2) 'To stay/remain': *fiquei em casa hoje*. (3) 'To become/get' with emotions and states — the default way to express change: *fiquei feliz* (I got happy), *ela ficou brava* (she got mad), *ficou pronto* (it's done/got ready). (4) The famously Brazilian social meaning: *ficar com alguém* = to hook up/make out with someone, no commitment implied — essential cultural vocabulary, and yes, *ficante* is the person you're casually seeing. When in doubt about 'get/become', reach for ficar.

examples.text: A padaria fica perto da minha casa.
examples.translation: The bakery is (located) near my house.

examples.text: Fiquei em casa o dia todo.
examples.translation: I stayed home all day.

examples.text: Ela ficou muito feliz com a notícia.
examples.translation: She got really happy about the news.
They hooked up at the party. (context makes the meaning; can also just mean 'they stayed')

commonErrors.wrong: A casa está na rua principal. (for a permanent location)
commonErrors.right: A casa fica na rua principal.
commonErrors.note: Not wrong, but ficar is the natural verb for where places are.
'tornar-se' is formal/written; spoken change-of-state is ficar.

drills.prompt: Say 'the house is on this street' (permanent location).

title: tem = there is/are (spoken) vs há (written)
explanationMd: To say 'there is / there are', spoken Brazil uses **tem** — the same verb as 'to have', always in the singular: *tem café?* (is there coffee?), *tem duas padarias na rua* (there are two bakeries on the street). The textbook form **há** is real but lives in writing, news, and formal speech; leading with *há* in a bar sounds like a butler. In the past, spoken 'there was/were' is **tinha** (*tinha muita gente*) or *teve* for events (*teve festa ontem* — there was a party). One shared rule: existential tem/há never pluralizes — *tem muitas casas*, never *têm muitas casas*. Bonus: há also means 'ago' in writing (*há dois anos*), which speech replaces with... *tem*: *tem dois anos que moro aqui*.

examples.translation: Is there fresh bread today?
There are two pharmacies on this street. (tem stays singular)

examples.text: Tinha muita gente na casa dela.
examples.translation: There were a lot of people at her house.
Vacancies available. (há survives in signs and formal writing)

commonErrors.note: Existential tem is always singular, whatever follows it.
Grammatically fine but socially off — speech uses tem.

drills.prompt: Ask 'is there milk?' the spoken way.

title: Comparatives and superlatives (mais que, o mais, melhor/pior)
explanationMd: Comparison is refreshingly regular: **mais ... (do) que** = more/-er than (*mais barato que*), **menos ... (do) que** = less than, **tão ... quanto** = as ... as. In speech *do que* and *que* are interchangeable; *que* alone is more common. The superlative just adds the article: **o/a mais ...** — *o café mais barato da cidade* (note: 'in the city' becomes **de/da**, not em). Four irregulars to memorize because their 'mais' versions are wrong: **melhor** (better, never *mais bom*), **pior** (worse), **maior** (bigger, never *mais grande*), **menor** (smaller). Everyday intensifier bonus: the absolute superlative -íssimo (*caríssimo* — super expensive) is common and slightly dramatic, which is exactly how Brazilians like it.

examples.text: O ônibus é mais barato que o táxi.
examples.translation: The bus is cheaper than the taxi.

examples.text: Esse é o melhor pão da cidade.
examples.translation: This is the best bread in the city. ('in' → da)

examples.text: Minha casa é menor que a sua.
examples.translation: My house is smaller than yours.

examples.text: O café daqui é bom demais.
examples.translation: The coffee here is really good. ('demais' = spoken intensifier)

commonErrors.note: bom/mau have irregular comparatives: melhor/pior.
grande → maior. (But 'mais pequeno' is accepted alongside menor.)
After a superlative, 'in' is expressed with de.

drills.answer: Água é mais barata que café.

drills.prompt: Say 'the biggest house on the street'.

title: Adverbs of frequency and time — what to say and where to put it
explanationMd: The core set: **sempre** (always), **nunca** (never), **às vezes** (sometimes), **geralmente/normalmente** (usually), **todo dia** (every day), **de vez em quando** (once in a while), plus time anchors **hoje, ontem, amanhã, agora, já, ainda**. Placement is loose — Portuguese is far more flexible than English — but the natural defaults are: sempre/nunca right before the verb (*eu sempre como pão*, *ela nunca bebe café*) and time expressions at the start or end of the sentence (*hoje eu vou lá / eu vou lá hoje*). Note that **nunca** before the verb needs no não (*nunca fui*), but after the verb it does (*não fui nunca*). Spoken favorites: **direto** (all the time — *ele viaja direto*) and **vivo/vive + gerund** (*ela vive reclamando* — she's always complaining).

examples.text: Eu sempre bebo café de manhã.
examples.translation: I always drink coffee in the morning.

examples.text: Ela nunca come pão à noite.
examples.translation: She never eats bread at night.

examples.text: Hoje a gente vai na casa do meu amigo.
examples.translation: Today we're going to my friend's house.
He talks about that all the time. (spoken 'direto')

commonErrors.wrong: Eu bebo sempre nunca café. (double frequency)
commonErrors.note: Pick one frequency adverb; nunca before the verb replaces não.
Pre-verbal nunca doesn't take não.

drills.prompt: Say 'I always speak Portuguese at home'.
drills.answer: Eu sempre falo português em casa.

drills.prompt: Say 'today I'm going to eat out'.

title: Diminutives -inho/-zinho — small words, big social force
explanationMd: The diminutive is grammatically simple — add **-inho/-inha** (words ending in unstressed -o/-a: *café → cafezinho*? no: *carro → carrinho*, *casa → casinha*) or **-zinho/-zinha** (words ending in stressed vowels, nasals, or consonants: *café → cafezinho*, *pão → pãozinho*, *mãe → mãezinha*). But its real function is social, and Brazilians deploy it constantly: it softens requests (*só um minutinho* — just a tiny minute), signals affection (*amorzinho*), makes offers warmer (*aceita um cafezinho?* — the national hospitality ritual), downplays (*um probleminha* — a little problem, often a big one), and intensifies with time words (*rapidinho* = really quick, *agorinha* = right now, *pertinho* = super close). It also attaches to adjectives and adverbs, not just nouns: *bonitinho*, *devagarinho*. Reading the diminutive is reading Brazilian social temperature — irony included: *joguinho* said flatly about a bad match is pure sarcasm.

examples.translation: Would you like a little coffee? (the classic Brazilian offer)
Wait just a sec, ok? (softened request)
I live really close by, I'll be there in no time. (intensifying, not literally 'small')
Will you buy me a bread roll? (pão → pãozinho, warm and standard)

commonErrors.note: Stressed final vowel → use -zinho.
With time/degree words the diminutive intensifies rather than diminishes.

drills.prompt: Offer someone a coffee the warm Brazilian way.
